message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Patients admitted to hospital with COVID-19 need rapid identification and isolation to prevent nosocomial transmission. However, isolation facilities are often limited, and SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR results are often not available when discharged from the emergency department. We evaluated a triage algorithm to isolate patients with suspected COVID-19 using simple clinical criteria and the FebriDx assay.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Retrospective observational cohort</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Large acute care hospital in London, UK</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    All medical admissions from the ED between 10
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    August 2020 and 4
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    November 2020 with valid SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interventions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Medical admissions were triaged as likely, possible or unlikely COVID-19 based on clinical criteria. Patients triaged as possible COVID-19 underwent FebriDx lateral flow assay on capillary blood, and those positive for MxA were managed as likely COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Primary Outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity and predictive values) of the algorithm and the FebriDx assay compared to SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR from nasopharyngeal swabs as the reference standard.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>4.0% (136/3,443) of medical admissions had RT-PCR confirmed COVID-19. Prevalence of COVID-19 was 45.7% (80/175) in those triaged as likely, 4.1% (50/1,225) in possible and 0.3% (6/2,033) in unlikely COVID-19. Compared to SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR, clinical triage had sensitivity of 95.6% (95%CI: 90.5% - 98.0%) and specificity of 61.5% (95%CI: 59.8% - 63.1%), whilst the triage algorithm including FebriDx had sensitivity of 92.6% (95%CI: 86.8% - 96.0%) and specificity of 86.4% (95%CI: 85.2% - 87.5%). The triage algorithm reduced the need for 2,859 patients to be admitted to isolation rooms. Ten patients missed by the algorithm had mild or asymptomatic COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A triage algorithm including FebriDx assay had good sensitivity and was useful to ‘rule-out’ COVID-19 among medical admissions to hospital.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>STRENGTHS AND LIMITATIONS OF THIS STUDY</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Pragmatic study including a large cohort of consecutive medical admissions providing routine clinical care.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>A single SARS-CoV-2 RT-PCR is an imperfect reference standard for COVID-19. Multiple RT-PCR platforms used, with different PCR targets and performance.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>A higher prevalence of COVID-19 or other respiratory pathogens might alter performance.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Criteria for likely and possible COVID-19 groups changed subtly during the study period.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Use of the FebriDx point-of-care assay as part of a triage algorithm for medical admissions with possible COVID-19

reference.unstructured: NHS England. Pillar 1 NHS labs COVID-19 testing turnaround time data: 15 July 2020. 2020. https://www.england.nhs.uk/coronavirus/publication/nhs-labs-covid-19-esting-turnaround-time-data/

National Institute for Health and Care Excellence. Overview | FebriDx for C-reactive protein and myxovirus resistance protein A testing | Advice | NICE. https://www.nice.org.uk/advice/mib224/ (xaccessed 19 Dec 2020).

Public Health England. COVID-19: infection prevention and control guidance. https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/sites/default/files/2020-10/COVID-19_Infection_prevention_and_control_guidance_complete.3.2%2818_06_2020%29_0.pdf

British Society of Thoracic Imaging.
